sections.about: This metric measures water procured from third-party suppliers such as municipal, industrial or utility providers under BRSR Principle 6 Essential Indicator water withdrawal by source. High third-party dependence may indicate pressure on shared infrastructure or purchased water reliance. A lower value indicates better performance.
sections.performanceAndDrivers: Tata remains the worst performer in FY2025 with 10.30 million kL of third-party water, up from 3.97 million kL in FY2024. Jindal improves to zero in FY2025 from 31,700 kL in FY2024; SAIL remains zero; JSW introduces 245,175 kL in FY2025 after zero in FY2023/FY2024. Tata’s higher value may reflect boundary-specific use of municipal wastewater, third-party utilities or site water supply arrangements; the BRSR context says Tata reuses treated municipal wastewater as part of its water strategy, which can be positive from a freshwater-substitution perspective even though the metric is directionally ranked lower-is-better.
sections.tataPositioning: In FY2025, Tata ranks 4th. SAIL and Jindal are best at zero, so percentage gap is not meaningful; Tata’s absolute gap is 10.30 million kL. Tata’s rank remains worst across the three years, and FY2024-25 worsens materially.
sections.targets: Tata’s BRSR identifies reuse of treated municipal wastewater and maximising internal recycling as part of water scarcity mitigation, but no third-party-water quantitative target was identified.
sections.comparabilityNotes: Tata Steel FY2022-23 third-party water withdrawal is reported for Tata Steel India and its Indian subsidiaries, not on a purely standalone basis. Third-party water can include treated municipal wastewater, which may be strategically beneficial if it substitutes freshwater; ranking should therefore be read with source-quality context.
